title: Cardiac Monitor (End Tidal CO₂)
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- All patients with an advanced airway insitu; LMA, ETT and surgical cricothyroidotomy.
- All patients receiving BVM therapy.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

Figure 2

- Locate the CO2 port on the cardiac monitor, right side (see **Figure 1**).

- Gently screw in CO2 detector (do not over tighten) (see **Figure 2**)

- Connect CO2 detector directly to the advanced airway or face mask.

Figure 3

Figure 4

- Locate CO2 soft key displayed on the screen (see **Figure 3**)

- Confirm CO2 device is on and pleth display is on screen (see **Figure 4**).

- If no waveform appears, **ensure patient is being ventilated, perform direct visualization/ laryngoscopy if any doubt about airway patency**, check the connection at the port, ensure that the CO2 detector is screwed in all the way and is ON and connected correctly to the ventilation device.

#### Contraindications
- Nil when indicated.

#### Complications
- If successful placement has been confirmed via multiple checks, and a sudden loss of capnography occurs then consider equipment troubleshooting if: 
- The patient continues to have cardiac output
- There has been no movement/extrication of the patient i.e. the airway device is unlikely to have dislodged
- The patient is otherwise stable
- Saturations remains stable at >90% SpO2 (remember SpO2 can be delayed).
- Troubleshooting should start at the patient end and move back towards the monitor.
- If a sudden loss of capnography occurs and: 
- the patient desaturates 2; **or**
- deteriorates quickly (with no loss in cardiac output) **and**- an adequate waveform does not return within 60-90 second of troubleshooting, then... 
**Do not attempt further equipment troubleshooting – remove the advanced airway and attempt reinsertion. Replace with an LMA or OPA/NPA, or surgical airway as a last resort.**

**Note:** the Zoll-X Series has a self-purge function with status message “SMF in-progress” where a dotted line will appear for approximately 10 seconds. This is a normal function.

#### Specific Notes
- Refer to Capnography.
- A large pulmonary embolus can cause a low CO2 reading.
